hero_sub: Voice, video, screen sharing, and messaging — all in one place. Lightweight, fast, and open source.
sc1_desc: Clean, familiar interface. Channels on the left, chat in the middle, members on the right. No learning curve — you already know how to use it.
sc2_desc: Jump into a voice channel and talk. Low latency, clear audio, no weird echoes. It sounds the way voice chat should sound.
sc3_title: Share your screen in full HD
sc3_desc: Stream at 1080p 30fps with system audio included. Show your friends what you're playing, present a project, or watch something together.
sc4_desc: DMs that stay between you and the other person. End-to-end encrypted when you want it, instant when you need it.
feat_desc: Built for real conversations — not for selling your data.
f1_desc: Crystal clear voice and video calls. Join community channels or start private conversations. Powered by LiveKit.
f2_desc: Share your screen in voice channels. Perfect for presentations, pair coding, or watching content together.
f3_desc: Messages, files, and calls are encrypted so only you and your recipients can read them. Not even the server has access.
f4_desc: No email, phone number, or ID verification required. Pick a username and you're in.
f5_desc: Fine-grained role system with per-channel permission overrides. Full control over who can do what.
f6_desc: Run your own server with a single command. Your data stays on your hardware. No Docker required.
f7_desc: Private conversations with friends. Add friends, send DMs, and make private calls — all encrypted.
f8_desc: Every line of code is public. Audit it, contribute to it, or fork it. Full transparency.
f9_desc: Go backend, minimal footprint. Works in the browser or as a desktop app on Windows, macOS, and Linux.
comp_desc: How we compare to the alternatives.
sh_desc: Run our setup script and it handles everything — firewall, credentials, LiveKit. No Docker, no coding experience needed.
guide_s1_desc_linux: You need a server (a computer that's always online). You can rent one from these providers for $3–5/month. Pick any provider, create the cheapest plan, and choose Ubuntu or Debian as the operating system.
guide_s1_desc_windows: You can use your own Windows PC as a server, or rent a Windows VPS. If you use your own PC, it needs to stay on and connected to the internet.
guide_auto_desc_linux: Connect to your server via SSH, then run this single command. The script downloads LiveKit, opens firewall ports, generates secure credentials, and starts it as a service — all automatically.
guide_auto_desc_windows: Open PowerShell as Administrator, then run this command. The script downloads LiveKit, opens firewall ports, generates secure credentials, and sets it up to start automatically.
guide_auto_output: When the script finishes, you'll see 3 values:
guide_auto_note_windows: The script sets up LiveKit to start automatically when your computer boots.
guide_connect_desc: Go to mqvi, create a new server, and select "Self-Hosted". Enter the 3 values the script gave you:
guide_connect_tip: That's it! mqvi will use your LiveKit server for all voice and video. Your conversations never touch our infrastructure.
guide_ssl_title: Important: SSL Required for Web Voice
guide_ssl_desc: If your LiveKit server doesn't have SSL (WSS), voice and video will only work through the desktop app. Web browsers require a secure connection (HTTPS/WSS) for microphone and camera access. To enable web voice, put a reverse proxy like Caddy in front of LiveKit to get automatic SSL.
guide_trouble_a1: Ports are probably closed. Run 'sudo ufw status' (Linux) or check Windows Firewall. Also check your cloud provider's web firewall if applicable.
guide_trouble_q2: I can connect but hear no audio
guide_trouble_a2: UDP ports 50000-60000 might be blocked. These carry the actual voice data. Make sure your provider allows UDP traffic on these ports.
guide_trouble_a3: LiveKit might not be running. On Linux, run 'systemctl status livekit'. On Windows, check Task Manager for 'livekit-server'. Try restarting with 'systemctl restart livekit' (Linux) or re-running the script.
guide_trouble_q4: Works on LAN but not from outside
guide_trouble_a4: Make sure 'use_external_ip: true' is set in your livekit.yaml. On Windows, also check that your router forwards ports 7880, 7881, 7882, and 50000-60000.
faq_a1: Yes. mqvi is open source and free to use. Paid plans may be introduced in the future, but core features will always remain free.
faq_a2: An open-source communication platform built with privacy in mind. Messaging, voice & video calls, screen sharing — all in one place.
faq_a3: End-to-end encryption for messages and calls, no analytics or trackers, and the source code is fully open for anyone to audit.
faq_q4: What platforms does it run on?
faq_a4: Web, Windows, macOS, and Linux. Mobile apps are currently in development.
faq_q5: Does it have voice and video chat?
faq_a5: Yes — voice channels, video calls, and screen sharing with system audio. Powered by LiveKit.
faq_a6: Every line of code is on GitHub. You can audit it, contribute, or fork it and run your own independent instance.
faq_a7: Yes. You can request account deletion from the app settings. There's a 14-day grace period in case you change your mind.
faq_q8: How do I report a bug?
faq_a8: Open an issue on our GitHub repository or use the in-app feedback feature.
cta_desc: Open source, no tracking. Just sign up and start talking.
ss_subtitle: Windows may show a SmartScreen warning. This is completely normal and does not mean the app is unsafe. It simply means we haven't paid Microsoft for a code signing certificate.
ss_step1: When you see this screen, click "More info" to reveal the install option.
ss_step2: Then click "Run anyway" to start the installation. That's it!
ss_github_note: mqvi is fully open source. You can review every line of code on
